turns.text: Hello Billie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for asking. It feels... strange, trying to put it into words while the memory of that drive is still so sharp. Everything about that morning was just this overwhelming mix of adrenaline and this crushing, quiet pressure. 

When I walked into the hospital, I felt like the staff was looking right through me. They saw the "nineteen" and the "unmarried" box I checked, and I could practically feel the assumptions settling over me like a heavy blanket. It’s isolating, honestly. I’ve spent so much time in clinical rotations, learning the rhythm of a delivery room, but when you’re the one actually experiencing it, the vocabulary just feels... inaccessible. 

I kept trying to find the right moment to bring up the Hepatitis B vaccine—that prophylaxis window is so narrow, and it’s all I could think about, even with the contractions coming on. I wanted to tell the midwife, "I know the protocol, I know why timing matters," but I felt like if I spoke up too much, they’d just label me as 'difficult' or 'anxious' instead of informed. It’s hard to be taken seriously when you look like just a kid. I just wanted them to see that I knew what my baby needed, even if I wasn't sure I knew how to be a mom yet.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I spent so many nights with my laptop open, carefully drafting that birth plan. I treated it like a clinical care plan—organized, logical, based on everything I’d studied in my nursing classes. I thought if I laid out my preferences clearly, the staff would look at me and think, "Okay, this girl knows what she's doing." I expected it to be a collaborative tool, a bridge between me and the nurses to show that I was a partner in my own care.

But when I handed it over, it felt like the midwife didn't even read it. She just glanced at the paper, gave me that pitying, tight-lipped smile, and tucked it into a folder. It didn't feel like a roadmap for my birth anymore; it felt like a piece of paper that made me look like I was trying too hard to control something they’d already decided for me. 

In the heat of labor, it felt completely useless. Nobody asked about my preferences for monitoring or movement. My voice felt so small against the hum of the machines and their clinical pace. I realized then that my "plan" was just a document, and in that room, the power dynamic was already set in stone. They were driving, and I was just along for the ride, hoping they wouldn't miss the exits that mattered most to me.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It happened during the transition phase. Everything was moving fast, and the pain was… it was intense, but I was still trying to keep my head clear. I had explicitly written that I wanted delayed cord clamping and that immediate skin-to-skin was my priority—I know the science behind the temperature regulation and the bonding, and it was the one thing I felt I could control for him.

When he finally arrived, I reached out, expecting them to place him on my chest. Instead, there was this flurry of activity, and before I could even catch my breath, they whisked him over to the warmer. They didn't explain why, they didn't ask, they just did it. I heard him crying—that beautiful, frantic sound—but he was all the way across the room. I felt this sharp, physical tug in my chest, worse than the labor. I tried to speak, to ask for him, but my voice was shaky and exhausted, and they were so busy with their own checklists that I just felt invisible. 

Watching them handle him while I lay there, feeling so exposed and empty-handed, was devastating. It felt like they’d decided my knowledge didn't matter, and my role was just to provide the baby and then get out of the way. I knew the protocol they were following, but being on the other side of it? It felt like my agency was being stripped away at the exact moment I needed it the most.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I suppose I expected a conversation. In my clinical training, we’re taught that pain management is a shared decision-making process—you explain the risks, the benefits, and the alternatives, and you let the patient decide what fits their threshold. I thought I’d be able to voice my preference for non-pharmacological methods first, like hydrotherapy or specific positioning, because I really wanted to stay present.

But the reality? It wasn’t a conversation; it was a sales pitch. As soon as the contractions started getting real, the nurse didn't ask me how I was coping or offer me a labor ball or a change of environment. She just kept hovering with the epidural talk. Every time I said I wanted to wait, she’d give me this look—like I was being a martyr for no reason or just being "young and stubborn." 

It felt like they were in a rush to get me "managed" so things would move faster. There was no patience for the physiological process of labor. They didn't support me in navigating the pain; they just offered a way to make me stop feeling it so I’d be easier to manage. I felt like a patient they needed to stabilize rather than a person they needed to support. It’s funny—I’m trained to help people through their hardest moments, and yet when I was in my own, I felt like my autonomy was the first thing they threw out the door.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted to try using the tub for labor. I’d read so much about the buoyancy and the way warm water helps with the intensity of contractions, especially during that early-to-mid stage. I thought if I could just get into the water, I’d be able to manage the physical sensation without needing to be stuck in a bed, tied to a monitor. 

But when I asked about it, the nurse just kind of waved it off. She mentioned something about "hospital policy" and "monitoring requirements" for someone my age—or maybe she just didn't want the hassle of setting it up for me. It felt like every request I made was met with an excuse about efficiency. They made it sound like I was asking for something radical, instead of just wanting a basic comfort measure that might have kept me from needing the heavy-duty interventions later. 

What really got in the way was just the attitude. There was no "How can we make this work for you?" It was just, "We don't do that here," or "It’s better if you stay in the bed." Once they decided I was a 'risk' or a 'teen mom,' I think they stopped seeing me as someone capable of making choices for my own body. They just wanted me in the bed where they could keep an eye on everything, and my comfort—or my birth experience—wasn't even on the list.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I think I expected it to feel like a clinic I’d be working in—bright, professional, but ultimately a place of support. I imagined a team that would see the nursing student in me, someone who respected the work I’d put in. I expected to feel, if not "welcomed," then at least treated like a person whose body was doing something miraculous. I wanted to feel safe, like I was in capable hands that wouldn't let anything happen to my baby.

But the actual environment? It felt cold. Not just the temperature—the air conditioning was freezing, which didn't help with the shivering—but the atmosphere was sterile and dismissive. The lighting was harsh, all overhead fluorescents that made me feel like an object under a microscope rather than a person laboring. 

When I walked in, I felt like a trespasser. Nobody looked me in the eye. It was all forms, intake signatures, and those quick, clinical glances at my belly. The silence between the staff was the worst part. They’d whisper things at the nurses' station and then go quiet when they walked into my room. It made me feel like I was a problem they were waiting to solve, not a person bringing a life into the world. It was lonely, and the reality was a million miles away from the "care" I’d been taught to provide.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise was just how *noisy* and chaotic it felt, even though it was physically quiet. You expect a hospital to be busy, but I didn't expect the sense of being "processed." 

I think I was shocked by the lack of personal space. I’m a nineteen-year-old girl walking into this room alone, scared out of my mind, and within minutes, people were just… appearing. It wasn't like the rounds I’d done where we knocked and introduced ourselves. It was just swinging doors and people hovering. 

The most jarring thing was the monitor. I’d spent so much time in class looking at the waveforms and the tracing, thinking of them as data, but hearing that steady *thump-thump-thump* of my son’s heart while I was standing there in a paper gown made it feel like he was already being tracked more than he was being cared for. It wasn't a supportive atmosphere; it was a surveillance atmosphere. I felt like I was being watched for a mistake, rather than supported through a transition. I didn't expect to feel like a liability in my own labor room, but that’s exactly what the atmosphere screamed at me. I was just a chart, and a complicated one at that, because I was young and I was there by myself.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

In my nursing rotations, we were taught the "Informed Consent" standard: you stop, you explain the clinical indication, you lay out the risks of both proceeding and waiting, and you allow the patient time to process. I expected that if something went sideways, I would be treated as an intelligent person—someone who understood the "why" behind an intervention.

But when things shifted during my labor, it was like the concept of "consent" just evaporated. The room filled up, and suddenly there were orders being barked back and forth between the staff. It wasn't a conversation; it was a series of declarations. They didn't come to me; they came *at* me. They’d say something like, "We’re going to need to do this now," and before I could even get the words out to ask, "Why?" or "Can we try X first?", they were already moving. 

It was terrifying. It wasn't just that they were overriding my plan; it was that they were stripping away my agency while I was at my most vulnerable. I wasn't a participant in my care; I was a subject. They made decisions *over* me, not *with* me. Every time I tried to reassert myself, I was shut down with a, "Honey, we’re just doing what’s safest for the baby," as if my knowing the clinical data was an inconvenience to their process. It felt like being a child again, not a mother, and definitely not a future nurse.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

I absolutely did not have a choice. Looking back, it’s crystal clear: those decisions were done *to* me. When they decided on the interventions, it wasn't a collaborative moment; it was a unilateral maneuver. 

There was no "Informed Consent." There was just "Compliance." When I asked questions, I was met with that patronizing tone—the "don't worry your pretty little head" kind of response that really stings when you’ve spent your life studying the very things they’re doing to you. It’s like they couldn't wrap their heads around the fact that I actually understood the interventions they were performing. They treated my questions as evidence of being "hysterical" or "uncooperative," rather than evidence of being a patient who deserves to know what's happening to her body and her child.

It’s humiliating to be a future nurse, someone who knows the system, and still be treated like an uneducated teenager who doesn't deserve to be in the loop. I felt like a spectator in my own delivery room. Everything happened in this blur of authoritative commands and physical movement, and I was just the container they were working on. It’s a bitter pill to swallow, knowing that even with the knowledge I had, the power imbalance was so deep that it didn't even matter. I didn't have a voice; I had a bed number.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I honestly expected a human connection. I know the clinical side, but I also knew that birth is a massive, life-altering shift. I hoped for someone who would recognize that I was doing this without a partner—someone who would see that I was scared and maybe just offer a reassuring hand or a moment of calm. I expected them to be the anchor when things felt like they were slipping away.

But the reality was so cold. It felt like they viewed me as a series of tasks to be completed. If they touched me, it was always for a procedure—checking dilation, adjusting a strap, or starting an IV. There was no "emotional" presence. When I wasn't being actively poked or prodded, the midwife would just step out or stand by the monitor, checking her watch or chatting about lunch with another nurse. I felt completely isolated.

When I was in the thick of it, trying to manage the pain, I remember looking for anyone to make eye contact with, just to feel like I wasn't alone in the room. Instead, I mostly saw the back of their heads or the glow of computer screens. It was like I was a project they were managing rather than a person experiencing the biggest moment of my life. It really drove home how lonely this whole journey has been.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this image burned into my brain from all the textbooks and the clinical training: the "Golden Hour." I expected to hear him cry, have them immediately place him on my chest—skin-to-skin—so his heart rate and temperature could regulate against mine, and then just... being a mother for those first precious minutes. I thought there would be a quiet, sacred space where the nurse would help me initiate that first nursing session while he was still alert.

The reality was a stark, jarring contrast. The second he arrived, it felt like a tactical operation. There was no gentle hand-off. He was taken from the birth canal and immediately whisked away to that stainless-steel warmer. I could see them working on him, but I couldn't reach him. The room felt crowded with people who weren't there for me, and the noise of the equipment drowned out the quiet I’d been longing for.

Instead of bonding, I was left there staring at the ceiling, shivering, with a team of people doing things to me—the placenta delivery, the suturing—that felt clinical and detached. They didn't explain why he couldn't come to me; they just prioritized the protocols over the person. When they finally brought him over, wrapped tightly in a blanket like a little package, he was already exhausted, and the magic of that immediate, primal connection felt like it had been filtered through a dozen hands before it finally reached me. It wasn't the welcoming into the world I’d prepared for.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It’s the silence—or rather, the specific *kind* of silence—that stays with me. Even though the room was filled with the beep of monitors and the muffled chatter of the staff, it felt like there was a void right where the connection to my son should have been. 

What still haunts me is the moment they weighed him. They took him from the room for a few minutes to do the routine checks, and I was just left there, staring at the empty warmer, completely motionless. I remember thinking, *I’m a mother now, but I don’t feel like his mother.* I felt like a patient whose procedure was finally winding down. 

When they finally brought him back, I wanted to hold him so badly, but I was so drained and intimidated by the staff that I didn't even reach out right away. I waited for them to tell me it was okay to hold him. That—the fact that I felt I needed *permission* to touch my own child—is something I can’t shake. I’m a student of pediatric health; I know how vital that immediate skin-to-skin contact is for his development, his cortisol levels, and our attachment. Yet, they made me feel like an outsider observing a process, not the center of it. It feels like a piece of that first hour was stolen, and I’m still angry about it.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think I expected a textbook recovery—soreness, fatigue, maybe a few weeks of adjustment. In my rotations, I’d seen new mothers leave the floor, and I naively thought, "Okay, you heal, you sleep when the baby sleeps, and you get back to your life." I had all these plans for balancing my classes and working through the postpartum period. I thought it would be a manageable, linear path.

The reality hit like a freight train. It wasn't just the physical healing—which, honestly, was way more painful and debilitating than I’d prepared for—it was the isolation. I was in this quiet apartment, miles from any real support, and the sheer exhaustion felt like a weight I couldn't move. Every time I looked at him, I was terrified I’d do something wrong, or that I was missing some vital sign I should have caught because of my training. 

I’m struggling with the recovery because there’s no one here to swap with, no one to say, "I've got the baby for an hour so you can shower." Everything is on me. I thought I’d be stronger, but I’m just… depleted. I’m physically trying to mend while managing the emotional fallout of that birth experience, and it feels like I’m running a marathon on a broken ankle. It’s not just healing; it’s an endurance test I never signed up for.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I thought feeding would be the one thing that was just... intuitive. In the clinical classes, they emphasize that it's a "natural" process, and I figured I’d pick it up like I picked up blood pressure cuffs or infant assessments. I imagined sitting in a cozy chair, feeling that rush of oxytocin, and having a lactation consultant walk in and just give me a few pointers if I needed them. I expected it to be a bridge—the final piece of that bonding I felt robbed of in the hospital.

The reality was just… hard. It wasn’t a gentle, natural flow; it was a struggle of logistics and raw, throbbing pain. My nipples were cracked, he wasn't latching quite right, and every time I tried, it felt like I was failing a test I hadn't studied for. There was no "support" because I was alone in my apartment. I kept thinking, *I’m a nursing student, I know the mechanics of the let-down reflex, I know about positioning, so why can't I just get this right?*

It’s humiliating. I felt like a failure because the "theory" in my head didn't match the reality of my body or his. I’d sit there at 3:00 AM, crying because it hurt so much, feeling completely unequipped, wishing someone was there to just tell me it was okay to be struggling. Feeding isn't just about nutrition; it's about comfort, and right now, it feels like just another source of stress.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

The gap was like a canyon. In the hospital, the support was all about "the mechanics"—they’d come in, watch me attempt a latch for thirty seconds, give me a quick, clinical instruction on positioning, and then leave. It was all about whether or not the baby was "getting enough" or if my latch was technically correct. They were looking at the data, not at me.

What I actually needed was someone to sit with me and acknowledge the *emotional* weight of it. I didn’t just need someone to fix the latch; I needed someone to say, "This is incredibly hard, you’re doing this alone, and it’s okay to be exhausted." I needed someone to hold space for the fact that every time I fed him, I was reminded of the birth—of how I felt like an object on a table instead of a mother.

When I got home, that support evaporated entirely. There was no one. The isolation turned feeding into this lonely, painful chore. I had all the clinical knowledge in the world about caloric intake and supply, but zero support for the actual, messy, emotional reality of being a nineteen-year-old mom feeding her baby in the dark. It’s not just about the breast or the bottle; it’s about having someone to say, "You're doing a good job," when you feel like you’re completely failing. They offered me technical tips, but I needed a lifeline.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I thought I’d be prepared for the "baby blues." I knew the symptoms: the mood swings, the weeping, the exhaustion—it was all in my textbooks. I figured I would just recognize them for what they were—hormonal shifts—and tell myself, "Okay, this is just science, it’ll pass." I expected to be the calm, observant nursing student-turned-mom, taking it all in stride.

The reality? It wasn’t a textbook case of "blues." It felt more like a total identity collapse. I didn't feel like a person anymore; I felt like a machine that was running on empty, constantly vibrating with this low-level, high-frequency panic. Every time he cried, I didn’t just feel concern; I felt this intense, physical jolt of "Did I miss something? Is he sick? Is it my fault?"

The loneliness is the part that hits hardest. At night, when I’m staring at the ceiling in the dark, the silence of this apartment feels like it’s pressing in on me. I’m haunted by the way they treated me at the hospital—that dismissive, clinical indifference—and it makes me doubt my own instincts. I keep wondering if I’m actually doing a good job or if I’m just faking it, waiting for someone to come in and tell me I’m not qualified for this. I’m not just tired; I’m grieving the version of myself I thought I’d be.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

The gap is huge. Because I’m a student of nursing, I think there’s this unspoken expectation that I’m supposed to be "resilient" and "clinical" about it all. If I express how terrified I am, or how much the isolation feels like it’s choking me, people look at me like, "But you know the science, you know what’s supposed to happen, why are you struggling?" It’s like my medical knowledge is a trap—it makes everyone assume I don't need the kind of hand-holding or emotional softness that a "regular" teenager might get.

I feel like I’m not allowed to be a kid who just became a mother. I’m expected to be this perfectly informed, calm, maternal professional, even when I’m trembling. If I tell a professional I’m struggling, I’m terrified they’ll see it through a clinical lens—that they’ll just add a code to my chart or look at me like a "case" of postpartum anxiety instead of a nineteen-year-old human who is in over her head. 

I’m constantly performing "competence" so no one takes my baby away or judges my parenting, but inside, I’m just screaming for someone to drop the act and talk to me like a real person. I’m not allowed to be messy, I’m not allowed to be scared, and I’m definitely not allowed to just be a girl who is lost.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I think I expected this seamless, almost cinematic transformation. I figured I’d be "Billie," just with a baby on my hip. I thought my nursing ambitions, my plans for my career, and my independence would all just settle into this new shape. I expected that the "mother" part of me would just be an extension of who I was before—an added dimension of strength.

The reality? It’s like I was hit by a shockwave, and the person I was before the delivery room didn’t quite survive the impact. I feel like I’ve been split in two. There’s the "clinical" version of me—the one who checks his temperature, keeps track of his vaccination schedule, and monitors his every breath—and then there’s the girl who is still nineteen, who is so deeply lonely it hurts, and who feels like she’s just masquerading as an adult. 

I don't know who "Billie" is anymore. I’m just "the mom." I look in the mirror and I see someone who looks a little older, a little more worn, and I wonder if that girl who was excited about pediatric nursing and had her whole life ahead of her on a map is gone for good. Everything I thought defined me—my intelligence, my agency, my future—feels like it’s been swallowed up by this constant, relentless need to keep him safe from a world I no longer trust. I’m not just growing into a new identity; I’m mourning the one I lost in the cold light of that hospital room.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

The biggest part I’ve lost is my confidence as an *intellectual*. Before the hospital, I felt smart. I felt like I was building this really solid foundation—my grades, my clinical rotations, the way I could explain a procedure or understand a protocol. I felt like I was on a path to being a competent, respected professional.

But now? That knowledge feels like a burden. It’s like I see the world through a lens of risk and deficit because I know all the things that *can* go wrong. My nursing identity feels like it was used against me in that room, and now I can’t shake the feeling that my own curiosity or competence is seen as "sassy" or "difficult" by the system. I have to dial back my own voice just to be heard as a patient, and that’s physically painful for me. 

Socially, I’ve just… faded away. My friends are doing college, going to parties, or just *being nineteen*. I can’t relate to that anymore. I’m tied to this apartment by the nursing schedule and the sheer, bone-deep exhaustion. I’ve lost the casual, easy version of myself. I’m constantly "on duty" now, both for him and for a world that seems convinced I’m barely making it. It’s hard to remember what it felt like to be just *me*, without the constant, heavy pulse of "motherhood" and "clinical risk" overriding every single thought. I miss just being a girl who didn't have to worry about the entire world, just for one hour.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a sort of "decompression zone"—a place where I could bring my list of questions, not just about the baby’s weight or my stitches, but about the *experience* of the birth itself. I thought they would ask how I was sleeping, how the transition to single motherhood was feeling, and if I was carrying any of the trauma from that day. I expected them to be curious about *me* as a person, not just my physical recovery.

In reality, those appointments felt like being on an assembly line. They’re timed to the minute—*check the incision, check the baby’s reflexes, update the chart, hand over a pamphlet, and out the door.* 

When I tried to bring up the way the birth felt—the way my agency was ignored—they’d just offer a polite, tight-lipped nod and redirect me back to the physical checklist. There was no space for the emotional reality. It felt like they were only looking for "red flags" that would require a prescription or a referral, not for the cracks in my spirit that were widening every day. I’d walk in needing a human connection, and I’d walk out with a brochure on postpartum exercise or a recommendation for a feeding app. It was efficient, sure, but it was profoundly hollow. I didn't need more pamphlets; I needed someone to actually *hear* what that birth did to me.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I tried, a few weeks in, to explain how hard it was to even be in the same room where I’d given birth. I wanted to tell them that every time I walked near that hospital, or even just drove past the turnoff, I’d get these phantom symptoms—shaking, shallow breathing, like my body was stuck in that same moment of transition. I wanted someone to use the word "trauma" or at least acknowledge that it was a valid reaction to what happened in that room.

But when I brought it up, the GP just gave me that clinical, distracted look. She asked if I was sleeping and if I had any suicidal ideation. When I said "no"—because I’m not, I’m just haunted and overwhelmed—the conversation just… closed. She checked a box on her screen and started talking about my iron levels.

It felt like they were only looking for a crisis, something they could fix with a prescription, not for the deeper, quieter pain of being stripped of my voice. They didn't see the underlying fear that my experience had fundamentally changed my capacity to trust the medical system I was trying to enter as a nurse. They dismissed it as "just being a new mom," which felt like such a patronizing brush-off. It made me realize that unless I was in full-blown crisis, my feelings were just background noise to them.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I think I expected it to be a bridge back to "myself." I thought going back to work would remind me that I was a person with a brain, a career, and potential—that I wasn't just a mom in a lonely apartment. I imagined it would feel like putting on a clean uniform and stepping back into the person I was before the hospital room swallowed me whole. I expected to feel competent again, using my training and actually feeling like I belonged somewhere.

The reality was more like a physical ache. Leaving my son at daycare for the first time felt like I was tearing a piece of my own soul away. I was sitting at my desk—or working a shift—and my mind was constantly vibrating with, *Is he okay? Is he crying? Does the caregiver know how to handle his specific needs?* I felt like I was living a double life: trying to be the professional, the "nurse in training," while inside I was just a raw, anxious wreck.

The exhaustion was absolute, but the worst part was the feeling that I was failing at both worlds. At work, I felt distracted and slow; at home, I felt guilty for being gone. It wasn't the bridge back to my old self; it was a constant, exhausting balancing act on a tightrope, and I felt like I was constantly one step away from falling.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

The sheer logistics were a nightmare I wasn't prepared for. Before, I thought, "I'll just pump, I'll schedule daycare, I'll make it work." But when you’re nineteen and trying to navigate the system without a partner to share the load, every single hour is a tactical battle. 

Feeding was the hardest. Trying to find a private, clean, and *legal* space to pump during a nursing rotation—when the nurses' station is packed and the breakroom is just a hallway—made me feel like a nuisance all over again. I’d be in a supply closet, timing my pump to the second, terrified I’d be paged and have to choose between my milk supply and my clinical duties. It felt like I was constantly apologizing for having a body that still needed to feed my son.

Childcare was the other piece. It’s expensive, it’s rigid, and it’s heart-wrenching. I had to drop him off at an ungodly hour just to make it to my clinicals on time. Walking away from him while he was still half-asleep, knowing I wouldn't see him for twelve hours, felt like a betrayal. I thought I’d be "doing it all," but instead, I was just surviving. I was constantly running—running to the bus, running to the classroom, running to the pump, running to get him before daycare closed. I wasn't living; I was just trying not to drop the ball.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I have to be honest—this is the part of my story that’s the hardest to talk about, because the "partner" I thought I’d have turned out to be a ghost. When I was pregnant, I imagined us as a team. I pictured us trading off feedings, talking through the scary parts of the night, and building this little life together. I expected that even if things were hard, there would be another person in the trenches with me—someone to hold my hand through the fear of the unknown.

But the reality is that I’m doing this alone. I’m a single mother, and that wasn't how I planned for my life to look at nineteen. There is no "us" to share the caregiving, no "us" to talk through the exhaustion, and no one else to hold the space for the trauma of the birth. It’s just me, in the silence of my apartment, staring at the baby monitor.

When I see other couples at the pediatrician’s office or the park, I feel this sharp, stinging sense of grief for the support I don't have. Every decision—every vaccination choice, every sleepless night, every financial stress—is my burden alone. It makes the isolation so much more tangible. I don’t have a relationship dynamic to manage; I just have a solo act, and some days, the weight of that feels like it’s going to crush me.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise, honestly, was the silence where the partnership should have been. I keep coming back to that—the way it just… stopped existing. I spent so much of my pregnancy imagining a "we." I’d talk to my baby, telling him about his father, imagining the three of us in a house, sharing the load of midnight feedings and those small, terrifying realizations that come with being new parents. 

I think the biggest shock was realizing how quickly "we" turned into "I." When things got hard, there was no transition into a shared struggle; there was just an exit. I went from having someone to plan a future with to being entirely, completely alone in a matter of weeks. It wasn't a slow unraveling; it felt like the floor just dropped out from under me. 

I’m surprised by how much I still look for him in the quiet moments, even though I know he’s not there. I’ll see something the baby does, or I’ll have a moment of fear about a health concern, and I’ll reach for my phone to tell him—only to realize I’m the only one who’s going to hear it. It’s a profound, hollow kind of surprise. I didn't think I’d be this alone. I didn't think motherhood would be a solo survival mission.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I think I expected maternity care to be a bridge between the clinical world I was training for and the personal transition into being a mother. I truly believed that because I understood the protocols and the science, I would be treated as an equal—someone who was guided and supported rather than just managed. I expected a sense of safety, not just physically, but emotionally; I wanted to be "held" by the system while I learned how to hold my son.

The most significant gap is the total lack of humanity. I received technical medical care, but I felt completely invisible as a person. They saw the "unmarried teen" box on my chart, and every interaction after that was filtered through that bias. It wasn't just a failure of care; it was a failure of recognition. 

I received information, but I didn't receive understanding. I received procedures, but I didn't receive support. That gap—the distance between being a patient they were "processing" and a mother they were supposed to be "welcoming"—is the most painful part. It turned what should have been the most empowering transformation of my life into a lonely, isolating battle against a system that refused to see me. I’m still trying to find my way back from that.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, I would shift the entire system away from this rigid, checklist-driven "processing" and toward a model of **continuous, human-centered advocacy.** 

Right now, the system treats birth like a high-stakes clinical intervention that just happens to involve a human being. We need a system that prioritizes a "primary advocate" or a dedicated care partner—someone who stays with you from the first prenatal visit through the early postpartum days. Someone who doesn't just check vitals, but checks *you*. 

I want a system where a nineteen-year-old girl, or anyone truly, is seen as an active participant rather than a liability to be managed. If the system was designed to listen first—to ask, "What are you afraid of?" or "What do you need to feel safe today?"—instead of just reciting protocols, it would change everything. 

We need to stop training providers to only see patients as a set of risks or a chart of demographics. We need to train them to see the *person* behind the intake form. If the system were designed to honor the autonomy of the mother, even the ones who are young or alone, we wouldn't feel like we’re fighting our own care teams just to have a voice. I want a system that builds you up as a mother instead of breaking you down just to get the job done.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There is one thing. I want to say that even though I felt silenced, and even though the system treated me like a liability, my son is the brightest, most important thing I have ever known. I think there’s this assumption that because I had a "difficult" birth and a lonely postpartum, my bond with him is somehow compromised, or that I’m just "traumatized." 

But the reality is that every single day, I am relearning how to be strong for him. I am proving to myself that I am more than the box they checked on that intake form. I just wish the healthcare system understood that the way they treat a mother in those first hours ripples out for a lifetime. If they had just listened—if they had just treated me like a mother instead of a "risk factor"—I think I would have started this journey feeling empowered rather than like I was constantly swimming upstream. 

I’m still here, and I’m still studying, and I’m still taking care of him. I just wish I didn't have to be quite so resilient just to survive the care I was given. Thank you for listening to me today; it’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like my voice actually mattered in this context.
